hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'animum' — 'Animus' with -us is nominative, the subject
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'animi' — 'Animus' is nominative here
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'anime' — 'Animus' is nominative, not being addressed

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'animi' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'animi' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'animi' with -i is 2nd declension

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'aegrum' — 'aeger' is nominative, modifying the subject
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'aegro' — 'aeger' is nominative here
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'aegri' — 'aeger' is nominative, agreeing with 'Animus'

hints.nominative: While 'avaritia' could be nominative, here it is ablative of cause — the agent causing corruption
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'avaritiam' — 'avaritia' is ablative here
hints.vocative: Vocative is not used here — 'avaritia' is ablative of cause

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'avaritiae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'avaritiae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'avaritiae' with -ae is 1st declension

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'corrumpens' — 'corruptus' with -tus is perfect passive
hints.future active participle: Future active participle would be 'corrupturus' — 'corruptus' is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'corrumpendus' — 'corruptus' is perfect passive participle

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'corruptus est' translates to?

hints.to break: That would be 'rumpere' — 'corruptus est' comes from 'corrumpere' (to corrupt)

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'corrumpitur' — 'corruptus est' is perfect passive
hints.pluperfect passive indicative: Pluperfect passive would be 'corruptus erat' — 'corruptus est' is perfect
hints.perfect passive subjunctive: Perfect passive subjunctive would be 'corruptus sit' — 'corruptus est' is indicative

hints.rumpo, rumpere, rupi, ruptum: These are the parts of 'rumpere' (to break) — 'corrumpere' is its compound
hints.corripio, corripere, corripui, correptum: These are the parts of 'corripere' (to seize/snatch)
hints.corroboro, corroborare, corroboravi, cor: These are the parts of 'corroborare' (to strengthen)

translation: A sick mind was easily corrupted by greed
